perturbation.original_text: Termination of this agreement shall not affect any accrued rights or remedies to which either party is entitled. 8. DEEMED DELIVERY 8.1 Any notice to be given in connection with this agreement shall be in wri ting in English and shall either be delivered by hand or sent by first class post or fax, email or other electronic form:
perturbation.changed_text: Termination of this agreement shall not affect any accrued rights or remedies to which either party is entitled. 8. DEEMED DELIVERY 8.1 Any notice to be given in connection with this agreement shall be in wri ting in English and shall only be delivered by hand.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notice delivery to 'by hand' only is not commercially reasonable in today's business environment. This is a legal contradiction because forcing hand delivery can obstruct effective notification, particularly in situations requiring prompt action or dispute resolution where electronic means are more practical and legally accepted.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-SIGN Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The E-SIGN Act establishes that electronic signatures and records have the same legal validity as traditional paper documents. By limiting acceptable forms of notice to 'by hand' delivery, the modified text contradicts the E-SIGN Act, which promotes the use of electronic communication in business and legal transactions.

perturbation.justification: The modified text, by restricting notice delivery to 'by hand' only, contradicts the spirit and intent of the E-SIGN Act, which promotes the use of electronic communication in business and legal transactions. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the 'Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act', indicating its relevance. The modification removes the option for electronic notice, creating a legal contradiction because it limits legally accepted forms of communication.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of each party shall continue for a period of [ Insert number of years - this should be a realistic estimate of how long it is likely that the information that is to be disclosed will remain important to protect, e.g. 5 ] years from the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each party shall continue for a period of 99 years from the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Setting a confidentiality period of 99 years is unreasonably long and may be deemed unenforceable as an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially if the information is not genuinely a trade secret and the restriction unduly burdens competition.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 186 states that a promise is unreasonably in restraint of trade if its performance would be an unreasonable restraint of trade. A 99-year confidentiality clause is a very long time period, and a court could find it to be an unreasonable restraint of trade, particularly in industries with rapid technological advancements, as it limits the free flow of information and competition for an excessive duration.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: About | HeinOnline Law Journal Library | HeinOnline Law Journal Library | HeinOnline Please click here if you are not redirected within a few seconds. perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the confidentiality period to 99 years, which, as the Perturbation and Law Explanations state, is likely an unreasonable restraint of trade according to Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 186. This is supported by the explanation stating that such a long period limits the free flow of information and competition for an excessive duration, especially in industries with rapid technological advancements. The changed text creates a period that is highly susceptible to being struck down as unenforceable by a court.

perturbation.original_text: 8.2 A co mmunication sent according clause 8.1 shall be deemed to have been received: 8.1.1 if delivered by hand, at the time of delivery ; 8.1.2 if sent by pre -paid first class post, on the second day after posting; or 8.1.3 if sent by fax, email or other electroni c form, at the time of completion of transmission by the sender.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.2 A co mmunication sent according clause 8.1 shall be deemed to have been received: 8.1.1 if delivered by hand, at the time of delivery ; 8.1.2 if sent by pre -paid first class post, on the tenth day after posting; or 8.1.3 if sent by fax, email or other electroni c form, at the time of completion of transmission by the sender.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the deemed receipt of a first-class post to the tenth day after posting is unusually long. It is against what is commercially reasonable and can be an issue in legal context. If in Belgium, this would violate the Belgium Postal Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: According to Article 21 in Belgium Postal Act of 1991, a first-class domestic post must be delivered within 1-3 business days. Changing the first-class post to tenth days will cause a substantial delay in notification. This is a breach of the act, which guarantees efficient and timely delivery.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the deemed receipt time for first-class post to ten days after posting. The Law Explanation states that Article 21 of the Belgium Postal Act of 1991 mandates delivery within 1-3 business days. The changed text introduces a substantial delay that directly contradicts this requirement, leading to a breach of the act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the relevant law as the '21 MARS 1991. - Loi portant réforme de certaines entreprises publiques économiques', further confirming the existence of the law. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the legal requirement for timely delivery as mandated by Belgian law.

perturbation.original_text: 12.1 This agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its s ubject matter or formation including non -contractual disputes or claims shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Belgium . 12.2 Each party irrevocably agrees to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Belgium over any claim or matter arising under or in connection with this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.1 This agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its s ubject matter or formation including non -contractual disputes or claims shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of Belgium . 12.2 Each party irrevocably agrees to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of North Korea over any claim or matter arising under or in connection with this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the jurisdiction to North Korea can cause unenforceability due to lack of established and independent judiciary, fair trial standards, and judicial cooperation. This is a contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The principle of comity dictates that courts of one jurisdiction will respect the laws and judicial decisions of another jurisdiction as long as they are not inconsistent with the laws and policies of the forum state. Due to North Korea's legal and political system differing vastly from those of Belgium (and most Western nations) along with it's lack of respect for international law or human rights standards, a Belgian court might refuse to enforce a judgment rendered by a North Korean court, particularly if it violates fundamental principles of fairness or due process.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Hilton v. Guyot | 159 U.S. 113 (1895) | Justia U.S. Supreme Court Center Hilton v. Guyot, 159 U.S. 113 (1895) Overview Opinions Syllabus U.S. Supreme Court Hilton v. Guyot, 159 U.S. 113 (1895) Hilton v. Guyot Nos. 130, 34 Argued April 10, 1894 Decided June 3, 1895 159 U.S. 113 Syllabus A citizen and resident of this country who has his principal place of business here but has an agent in a foreign country and is accustomed to purchase and store large quantities of goods there, and, in a suit brought against him by a citizen and in a court of that country, appears and defends with the sole object of preventing his property within the jurisdiction, but not in the custody of that court, from being taken in satisfaction of any judgment that may be recovered against him there cannot, in an action brought against him in this country upon such a judgment, impeach it for want of jurisdiction of his person. The admission at the trial in a court of a foreign country, according to its law and practice, of testimony not under oath and without opportunity of cross-examination, and of documents with which the defendant had no connection and which by our law would not be admissible against him, is not of itself a sufficient ground for impeaching the judgment of that court in an action brought upon it in this country. When an action is brought in a court of this country by a citizen of a foreign country against one of our own citizens to recover a sum of money adjudged by a court of that country to be due from the defendant to the plaintiff, and the foreign judgment appears to have been rendered by a competent court, having jurisdiction of the cause and of the parties, and upon due allegations and proofs and opportunity to defend against them, and its proceedings are according to the course of a civilized jurisprudence, and are stated in a clear and formal record, the judgment is prima facie evidence, at least, of the truth of the matter adjudged, and the judgment is conclusive upon the merits tried in the foreign co
perturbation.justification: The changed text substitutes the jurisdiction of Belgium courts with those of North Korea. The Perturbation Explanation states that this creates unenforceability due to the lack of an established and independent judiciary, fair trial standards, and judicial cooperation in North Korea. The Law Explanation supports this, arguing that due to North Korea's vastly different legal and political system and disregard for international law and human rights, a Belgian court might refuse to enforce a North Korean court's judgment. Scraped Law Snippet 2 implies that judgments should be rendered by a competent court, with jurisdiction of the cause and of the parties, and upon due allegations and proofs and opportunity to defend against them, and its proceedings are according to the course of a civilized jurisprudence, for the judgment to be considered prima facie evidence. Given North Korea's judicial system is unlikely to meet these standards, this creates a clear legal contradiction.
